evacuating and caring for Fukushima citizens in the immediate aftermath of the disaster. The first author participated in disaster relief efforts in two fallout shelters in Fukushima. This article was written to share her post-disaster care experience and learned knowledge with medical care professionals in Taiwan and other high earthquake risk areas. The article is divided into three parts: (1) the disaster situation and the role of medical care professionals, (2) the health effects of radiation exposure, and (3) the challenges and lessons learned from the disaster relief efforts. The article concludes with a discussion on the importance of disaster preparedness and the need for medical care

Shojo Beat was cancelled in June2009 after a three-year run. The final issue was dated July2009.

Romans believed fauns inspired fear in men traveling in lonely, remote or wild places. They were also capable of guiding humans in need, as in the fable of The Satyr and the Traveller, in the title of which Latin authors substituted the word "faun" for the Greek "satyr". The Romans also had a tradition of depicting the faun as a dangerous and lustful figure who could inspire fear as well as friendly feelings.

Health Access: a locality-based model of health care navigation for the homeless.
The authors describe Charlottesville Health Access (CHA), an initiative to get people who are homeless into the health care system. A community homeless shelter worked with faculty and students from the Univ. of Virginia Schools of Medicine and Nursing to create a model of health care navigation. The model includes a nurse practitioner who provides on-site care at the shelter, a health educator who provides health education and outreach, and a health navigator who helps clients access health care services. The authors describe the development of the model, the services provided, and the outcomes of the program.
